examples: De tevreden poes ligt rustig te snorren na een visje te hebben opgepeuzeld.

tevreden.e: From Middle Dutch tevreden. Equivalent to a univerbation of te + vreden. Compare German zufrieden.

traan.e: From Middle Dutch trâen, from Old Dutch trān, from Proto-Germanic *trahnuz. Cognate with German Träne (“tear”) and Tran (“fish oil”).

examples: We moeten plannen maken voor de toekomst.

toekomst.e: From Middle Dutch toecomste (“arrival, visit”). Equivalent to toe + komst. Temporal use developed first in the adjective toekomstig. Such use of the noun (ca. 1800) appears to follow German Zukunft and the word is most often used since the 18th century and has been attested since the 14th century...

examples: Het gebit bestaat uit tanden, hoektanden en kiezen

tand.e: From Middle Dutch tant, from Old Dutch tant, from Proto-West Germanic *tanþ, from Proto-Germanic *tanþs, from Proto-Indo-European *h₃dónts.

telefoon.e: Borrowed from French téléphone, equivalent to: tele- + -foon.

s.def: to matter, to be of significance

tellen.e:
From Middle Dutch tellen, from Old Dutch tellen, from Proto-West Germanic *talljan, from Proto-Germanic *taljaną.
Equivalent to tal (“number”) + -en (“denominative”) or taal (“language”) + -en (“denominative”) and shows the effect of historical umlaut.

examples: Hij heeft geen tijd om te wachten.
We moeten meer tijd besteden aan het leren van nieuwe vaardigheden.
In deze zin wordt de tegenwoordige tijd gebruikt.
Hij heeft de werkwoordvorm naar de verleden tijd veranderd.

Proto-West Germanic *tīdi

trui.e: From Middle Dutch troye, from Middle Low German troie, troige, troge (“jacket, vest”) (also in Middle High German troie, treie, and Old Norse treyja (“jerkin, sleeveless jacket”)), from Old French troie (“doublet”), from Late Latin troja, troga (“horsehair monk's garment”), possibly originally of...

totaal.e: From Middle Dutch totael, from Middle French total, from Old French total, from Medieval Latin tōtālis, from tōtus (“all, whole, entire”).

s.def: a pie, large cake, or cobbler

taart.e: From Middle Dutch tāerte, from Old French tarte.

tamelijk.e: From Middle Dutch tamelijc, temelijc. Related to betamen, betamelijk. Compare German ziemlich.

s.def: at home (at one's dwelling or in one's home area)
examples: Ik ben niet thuis.
Ze zijn nog niet thuis.
Hoe maak je van een huis een thuis?

thuis.e: From Middle Dutch thuus, contraction of te huus; equivalent to modern te + huis.

examples: Er is een toename in de verkoop van elektrische auto's.

s.def: singular dependent-clause past subjunctive of toenemen

toename.e: Derived from toenemen on the model of German Zunahme. The same is true of other nouns in -name.

examples: Het tweede gesprek eindigde in het aanbieden van een baan.

tweede.e: From Middle Dutch tweede, tweeste, formed from twee by analogy with other ordinals. The original ordinal was ander.

s.def: Transvaal (a geographical region and former province of South Africa)
Transvaal (a former country in Southern Africa)
Tiber, the river that flows through Rome in Italy.
a Dutch, otherwise European or Chinese shop
examples: A shop for excellent DRINKS.

examples:

Opende de toko, sloot de toko, ik wóónde praktisch in die toko.

toko.e: From Indonesian Malay toko (“shop”), from Hokkien 土庫 /土库 (thó͘-khò͘, “private storehouse”).

a neighbourhood of Lelystad, Flevoland, Netherlands

tjalk.e: Borrowed from West Frisian tsjalk. Often considered a diminutive of an unattested Old Frisian cognate of kiel (“keel”).

s.def: a tulip, a plant of the genus Tulipa
a tulip, a flower of this plant

tulp.e: Borrowed from Middle French tulipe, from Turkish tülbent (“fine muslin, turban”), from Persian دلبند (dolband, “turban”). Doublet of tulband.

s.def: singular dependent-clause past indicative of thuiskomen

examples: Wanneer kom je thuis?
Het was een teleurstellend resultaat.
De verkoop was teleurstellender dan we hadden gehoopt.

typen.e: From English type. Compare German tippen.

s.def: tonnage (water displacement of a ship measured in tons)
tonnage (cargo capacity of a ship's hold)

tonnage.e: Borrowed from Middle French tonnage. Later influenced by English tonnage.

s.def: totem, object that signifies a kinship group
totem, physical representation of a kinship symbol

tribune.e: Borrowed from French tribune, from Italian tribuna.

s.def: a city in the province of Antwerp, Belgium
timing (synchronisation; regulation of pace or time)
migratory bird (sometimes specified as an actually migrating bird)
lead, driving force (person in charge of a project)

trekker.e: From Middle Dutch trecker. Equivalent to trekken + -er. The sense “tractor” is a phono-semantic matching of English tractor. Compare the same in German Trecker.

tricolon.e: Learned borrowing from Ancient Greek τρικωλον (trikōlon, “having three parts”).

toorn.e:
From Middle Dutch torn, from Old Dutch *torn, from Proto-Germanic *turnaz. Cognate with German Zorn.
Attested since 1240.

s.def: to time (measure or record the time, duration, or rate of something)
Trinidad and Tobago (a country consisting of two main islands and several smaller islands in the Caribbean, off the coast of Venezuela)
Turkey (a country located in Eastern Thrace in Southeastern Europe and Anatolia in West Asia)
someone or something excellent; a belter, a ripper

topper.e:
From top + -er.
The bird name is generally taken to derive from the noun top ("top"), not the adjective top ("great, amazing"). Some have instead adduced the dialectal word dobber (referring to both the greater scaup and the tufted duck), which was apparently current in Zeeuws-Vlaanderen at some ...

examples: Telkens wordt hij herkozen.
Hij onderbreekt me telkens.

telkens.e: From Middle Dutch telken, as a contraction of te elken, in phrases such as te elken male / telkenmale ("at each time"). Later, the adverbial -s was added.

examples: Telkens als ik op de deur klop, hoor ik iets raars.
Telkens wanneer ik dat hoor, denk ik terug aan toen.

troosten.e: From Middle Dutch trôosten, from Old Dutch trōsten, from Proto-Germanic *traustijaną. Cognate with German trösten.

tiende.e: From Middle Dutch tiende, from Old Dutch *tiando, from Proto-Germanic *tehundô.

examples: Je middelvinger zit tussen je wijsvinger en je ringvinger.
Als ik moest kiezen tussen jou en hem, zou ik altijd voor jou kiezen.
Ik kon je niet vinden tussen al die mensen.

tussen.e: From Middle Dutch twisschen, tusschen, a shortening of Old Dutch intwiskan, originally a prepositional phrase *in twiskan. The latter word is from Proto-Germanic *twiskaz, *twiskijaz (“twofold, double”), from Proto-Indo-European *dwís (“twice, doubly; in two”).

examples: Je moet die jongens echt even in toom houden

s.def: a flock of birds (especially ducks, geese and swans)
a hamlet in Cranendonck, North Brabant, Netherlands

toom.e: From Middle Dutch toom, from Old Dutch *tōm, from Proto-West Germanic *taum, from Proto-Germanic *taumaz. Doublet of team.

tegel.e: From Middle Dutch tigele, tegel, from Old Dutch tegela, from Proto-West Germanic *tigulā, from Proto-Germanic *tigulǭ, from Latin tegula. Compare German Ziegel, English tile.

tuig.e: From Middle Dutch tuyg (“tools, apparatus, utensil, ornament”), from Old Dutch *tiug, from Proto-West Germanic *teug, from Proto-Germanic *tiugiją, *teugą (“stuff, matter, device”), from *teuhaną (“to lead, bring, pull”), from Proto-Indo-European *dewk- (“to pull, lead”). Cognate with German Zeug...

tonder.e: From Middle Dutch tunder, from Old Dutch *tundar, from Proto-West Germanic *tundr, from Proto-Germanic *tundrą.

s.def: a hamlet in Maashorst, North Brabant, Netherlands

trent.e: First attested as op de Trent in 1727. Derived from Middle Dutch trent (“round object, disc, circle”).

s.def: a village in Steenwijkerland, Overijssel, Netherlands

tuk.e: See the etymology of the corresponding lemma form.

s.def: a male given name from Hebrew

tobias.e: Ultimately from Ancient Greek Τοβίας (Tobías), from Hebrew טוֹבִיָּה (toviyá, literally “my good is YHWH”).

examples: Het was een lange tocht, maar gelukkig zonder problemen.
Je voelt de tocht door deze kier komen.

s.def: a small waterway that connects all the other waterways in a polder to a pump, windmill or sluice

tocht.e: From Middle Dutch tocht or tucht, from Old Dutch *tuht, ultimately from Proto-Germanic *tuhtiz, cognate to German Zucht. Equivalent to tijgen (“to go, pull”) + -t (“verbal noun”). Doublet of tucht.

s.def: strips of parboiled and then fried cassava
a meal containing telo as a side dish, usually with a serving of a green vegetable such as yardlong bean, and a serving of fish, such as sprats or salt cod
a snack consisting of telo and a small serving of salt cod

telo.e: From Caribbean Javanese telo, from Javanese ꦠꦺꦭ (téla), a variant of ꦏꦠꦺꦭ (katéla), a word for non-native tuberous plants that was borrowed from Portuguese Castela and thus ultimately derives from Latin castellum, making this a doublet of ketella, kasteel, chateau, Castilië, Kessel, and castellum...

s.def: a tetrarch (governor or ruler of a quarter of a country)
a tetrarch (one of the four members of a jointly ruling tetrarchy)
a tetrarch (officer in charge of a fourth part of a phalanx in Ancient Greece)

tetrarch.e: Learned borrowing from Latin tetrarchēs, from Ancient Greek τετράρχης (tetrárkhēs), from τετρα- (tetra-, “four”) + -άρχης (-árkhēs, “ruler, leader”).

s.def: a particular gait of a horse, particularly Icelandic ones
an interpreter, one who translates/interprets speech in another language and vice versa
a sailors' term for a (folding) rule to measure bolt holes

tolk.e: From Middle Dutch tolic, from Middle High German tolk, from Old East Slavic тълкъ (tŭlkŭ), толкъ (tolkŭ), from Proto-Slavic *tъlkъ.

s.def: running board (step under the car door)
to harness, to put a harness on

tij.e: From older tijde, from getijde, which see.

examples: op korte termijn
op lange termijn

tombe.e: From Middle Dutch tombe, from Old French tombe, from Latin tumba, from Ancient Greek τύμβος (túmbos).

examples: Ze protesteerden tegen het nieuwe beleid.
Hij nam medicijnen tegen zijn hoofdpijn.
Ze liep tegen de deur aan.
De kat rende tegen het raam.
De uitslag was drie tegen één.

tegen.e: From Middle Dutch tegen, tjegen, from te jegen, the latter from Old Dutch gegin, from Proto-West Germanic *gagin, from Proto-Germanic *gagin. See also jegens.

examples: Tijdens de vakantie was ik ziek.
Zing en dans niet tijdens de rouwperiode.

tijdens.e: From tijd + -s, by analogy of words like nopens and wegens.

examples: Ze stonden tegen elkaar over.

tegenover.e: Compound of tegen + over. First attested in the 17th century.

terrarium.e: From Latin terra (“earth”) + -arium, by analogy with aquarium.

examples: Het leek alsof ik door een mokerslag was getroffen.

s.def: to find (by chance), hit upon, to encounter

examples: In de hoofdstad Quito zijn al enkele dagen demonstraties tegen bezuinigingen op de politiesalarissen, waarbij het tot gewapend treffen tussen het leger en de rebellerende agenten is gekomen.

treffen.e: From Middle Dutch treffen, from Old High German treffan, from Proto-West Germanic *drepan, from Proto-Germanic *drepaną. Replaced rare Middle Dutch drepen, from Old Dutch drepan, from the same Proto-Germanic source.

examples: Neem een stap terug, en ga dan naar rechts.

s.def: back, to a previous place or condition
examples: Geef dat terug!
Toen mijn vriend naar me zwaaide, zwaaide ik naar hem terug.
Een tijdje terug heb ik een huis gekocht.
Toen ze zag dat het nog donker was, viel ze terug in slaap.

terug.e: Univerbation of te (“to”) + rug (“back”); compare German zurück, Low German torüch.

s.def: the fin or sail of a submarine

toren.e: From Middle Dutch torre, from Old Dutch turn, from Old French tur, from Latin turris.

treurwilg.e: Compound of treuren (“to mourn”) + wilg (“willow”), so named for its downward-drooping branches.

tiet.e:
From Low German Titte (“nipple; breast”), from Middle Low German, ultimately from Proto-Germanic *titt- (“teat; nipple; breast”), from Proto-Indo-European *tata- (“father; parent; nipple”).
Related to German Zitze, English tit, teat.

examples: Hij is ziek, maar toch werkt hij door.

s.def: after all, despite what was expected
examples: Ach, je bent toch gekomen!

s.def: yes, surely; implies a positive contradiction, used to contradict a negative.
examples: Wil je niet meekomen? — Toch wel!

s.def: In positive statements, a modal particle indicating certainty or emphasis, implying that the speaker considers his statement to be obvious.
examples: Dat weet ik toch niet! Ik ben toch geen automonteur!

s.def: In questions, a modal particle indicating uncertainty or sometimes worry, implying that the speaker hopes to be confirmed or reassured; may also be postpositioned as a tag question.; right? is that so?
examples: Hij is toch gekomen? (with emphasis on gekomen)
Jij bent Gerrit, toch?

toch.e: From Middle Dutch toch, alteration of doch.

tafereel.e: Dissimilated from *tafeleel, from a diminutive of tafel (“table”).

teddybeer.e: Borrowed from English teddybear. Named after Theodore Roosevelt, who was nicknamed "Teddy".

s.def: a diminutive of the male given name Timotheüs

tomaat.e: Borrowed from English tomato, subsequently influenced by French tomate, both from Spanish tomate, from Classical Nahuatl tomatl (“tomatillo”).

s.def: magazine (periodical of which print instalments appear)

tijdschrift.e:
From tijd + schrift.
In use in the sense "periodical" since at least 1775 or earlier.
Possible calque of German Zeitschrift.

talen.e: From Middle Dutch tālen, from Old Dutch *talon, from Proto-West Germanic *talōn, from Proto-Germanic *talōną. Cognate with English tale.

talles.e: Borrowed from Yiddish טלית (tales), from Hebrew טַלִּית (tallít).

s.def: tyrant, absolute ruler of an Ancient polis

examples: Tirannen van Caligula tot Ceaucescu hebben grillen, wreedheid en grootheidswaan gemeen

s.def: any oppressive, bossy or dictatorial person
a tyrant flycatcher, any bird of the family Tyrannidae

tiran.e: From Latin tyrannus (“monarch, ruler; tyrant”), itself from Ancient Greek τύραννος (túrannos, “tyrant”). The sense "tyrant flycatcher" corresponds to the taxonomic family name, translingual Tyrannidae; more at tyrant flycatcher.

tomen.e: From Middle Dutch tômen, from Old Dutch *tōmen, from Proto-West Germanic *taumijan, from Proto-Germanic *taumijaną. Equivalent to toom (“briddle”) + -en.

s.def: to unpick; to loosen a stitch
examples: Het is moeilijk om deze naad te tornen zonder de stof te beschadigen.
Het is onverstandig om te tornen aan de vrijheid van meningsuiting.

tornen.e: From Middle Dutch ternen, from earlier trennen by liquid metathesis (compare Kerstmis (“Christmas”) and vorst (“frost”)), from Old Dutch *trennen, from Proto-West Germanic *trannijan, from Proto-Germanic *trannijaną.

trillen.e: From Middle Dutch trillen, further etymology unknown.

s.def: doormat; not very assertive person who lets others take advantage of them

toetje.e: Diminutive of toe. Literally, “[a] little something after”.

s.def: terror, instance or cause of fear

examples: De hond werd verdacht van betrokkenheid bij terreur.

terreur.e: Borrowed from French terreur f, from Middle French terreur f, from Old French terreur f, from Latin terror m.

examples: Dokwerker Jan C. voelde zich geprovoceerd door de opmerking van herbergierster Joanna M. dat ze tevreden was hem samen met zijn vriendin werkvrouw Anna W. te zien. Jan C. die blijkbaar al zijn vermoedens had over de lesbische contacten van zijn vriendin, antwoordde: ‘Zijt gij mijn wijf haar truttenlakker, gij geeft export voor al hetgeen er binnen is en honderd frank er boven over, of gij moogt mijn wijf haar trut niet meer uitlakken.’

trut.e: Unknown. Possibly an alteration of treuzelen (“to tarry”) or prut (“gunk”), or a combination of trui (“slow, ugly, or promiscuous woman”) and kut (“cunt”) and/or tut (“stiff woman”).

s.def: measure word, counter (class of word used along with numerals to count objects)
a tram stop (Commonwealth), a streetcar stop (North America)
to go; appended to noun phrases to indicate takeaway meals or takeaway establishments

tautologie.e: From Late Latin tautologia, from Ancient Greek ταὐτολογία (tautología) from ταὐτός (tautós, “the same”) + λόγος (lógos, “explanation”).

taiga.e: Ultimately from Russian тайга́ (tajgá), from Turkic or Yakut тайга (tayga), which roughly could be translated as "slippery place". Most likely, the meaning come from a verb Yakut тай (tay) - to slip.

s.def: Created verbs with a sense of ‘in pieces, apart, asunder’, or with intensive force.
examples: tekappen (“to hew into pieces”)
terijten (“to rip apart”)

te-.e: From Middle Dutch te-, from Old Dutch te-, from Proto-Germanic *twiz-, from Proto-Indo-European *dwis-. Compare German zer-.

triplex.e: Borrowed from Latin triplex. In the sense “three-veneer plywood” likely a shortening of triplexhout.

examples: We kijken eerst naar wat je tot nu toe hebt bereikt.

tevens.e: From Middle Dutch teffens. Equivalent to te- + even + -s.

s.def: tabletop exercise (An emergency exercise where participants come together in an informal setting in, for example, a meeting room, to discuss their roles during a disaster or crisis and to review relevant procedures based on one or more scenarios)
Used to express thinking or pondering; hmm, yeah well
examples: Tja, eigenlijk weet ik dat niet.
Waar liggen de autosleutels? — Tja, daar vraag je me wat.

s.def: Used to express ambivalence or resignation
examples: Een zeilboot is wel veel onderhoud, hoor. — Tja, dat hou je toch.
Zullen we morgen ergens gaan koffiedrinken? — Tja, of brunchen?
Zal ik een tatoeage laten zetten? — Tja. Dat moet je zelf weten.
Heb je de nieuwe Star Wars-film al gezien? — Tja, ik heb echt helemaal niets met sciencefiction.

s.def: Used to express that something is to be expected or anticipated
examples: Er zaten voornamelijk vijftigplussers in de zaal. — Tja, wat verwacht je bij een Bach-uitvoering?
Ik ben kletsnat geworden. — Tja, had je maar een regenjas moeten meenemen.

s.def: Used to indirectly express disagreement or disapproval
examples: Gerard Cox is een geweldige zanger. — Tja, vind je?
We hebben heerlijk gewandeld tijdens onze vakantie. — Tja, ik lig liever aan het strand.

tja.e: Variant of ja (“yes”). Compare tjonge (from jongen). Compare also Danish ja (“yes”) vs tja (“well…”); Finnish jaa (“yes; oh, well”), tjaa (“well…”).

examples: Zijn liefde voor de natuur komt terug in zijn schilderijen.

s.def: to go back on; to renege on
examples: Klager stelt dat werkgever op de gemaakte afspraak is teruggekomen.

s.def: to revisit (a topic); to pay renewed attention to
examples: We zullen in het volgende hoofdstuk nog uitgebreid op deze kwestie terugkomen.

s.def: moment in time, point in time, time of day
examples: Op dat tijdstip sliep ik nog.
Ondanks het late tijdstip was het nog erg druk op straat.
Kom maar op - tenminste, als je durft.

trouwens.e: From the obsolete trouwen (“indeed, really”) + -s (adverbial suffix). The adverb trouwen was the petrified dative singular of the noun trouw (“loyalty”) in its obsolete sense “word of honour”.

s.def: then, subsequently (at a time in the past)
examples: Toen ging hij weg.

s.def: back then, at the time, in those days
examples: Toen was dat nog heel gewoon.

s.def: when, at the time that (in the past)
examples: Hoe oud was je toen je vegetariër werd?
Toen João in 1821 terugkeerde naar Portugal bleef zijn zoon Peter als regent achter in Brazilië.

toen.e:
From Middle Dutch doe, doen (with -n from dan), from Old Dutch thuo, from Proto-West Germanic *þō.
Cognate with Luxembourgish dunn and similar forms (do, du, dun) in German dialects.

examples: Groot is Uw trouw, o Heer.

trouw.e: From Middle Dutch trouwe, from Old Dutch triuwa, from Proto-West Germanic *treuwu, from Proto-Germanic *trewwō.

taak.e: 16th century, from Picard take, from Old Northern French tasque, variant of Old French tasche, from Vulgar Latin tasca, from older taxa, deverbal from Latin taxare. Cognate with English task, French tâche.

tonen.e:
From Middle Dutch tônen, from Old Dutch *tōnen. Cognate with Middle Low German tônen, Middle High German zounen, zōnen.
Probably a byform of synonymous Middle Dutch tôghen, cognate with Old Saxon tōgian, Old High German zougen, Gothic 𐌰𐍄𐌰𐌿𐌲𐌾𐌰𐌽 (ataugjan), derived from Proto-Germanic *augô (“eye”)...

examples: Tolerantie begint met de erkenning dat iemands waarheid nooit voor iedereen dé waarheid kan zijn.

toedoen.e:
From toe (“towards”) + doen (“to do”).
Cognate to German zutun

terrine.e: Borrowed from French terrine, from Middle French terrine.

s.def: execution, the act of carrying out a death sentence

tussenwerpsel.e: From tussenwerpen + -sel. Calque of Latin interiectiō.

s.def: while, during the same time that

examples: Tussen de geslachten bestaat een groot verschil in gewicht; mannetjes wegen gemiddeld ongeveer 100 gram, terwijl vrouwtjes tot 160 gram kunnen wegen.

terwijl.e: From now-obsolete terwijlen, from Middle Dutch terwilen, a shortening of ter wilen dat (“at the moment that”). See also wijl.

examples: Het aantal werklozen neemt toe.

toenemen.e: From Middle Dutch toenemen. Equivalent to toe + nemen.

s.def: to work out, especially to jog
to trim the hair of a dog or a horse

trimmen.e: Ca. 1900, from English trim, from Middle English trimmen, from Old English trymman. The sense “work out” perhaps after the English expression keep in trim. This sense appears simultaneously in Dutch and German in the later 1960s, so one may have borrowed it from the other.

examples: Zit ni zo in diejen trok, sebiet schaarde 'n valling oep!

trok.e: Derived from trekken (“to pull; (air flow) to draft”).

examples: aangeslagen toestand
grondtoestand

toestand.e: From Middle Dutch toestand, from toestaen (“to assist”) (modern toestaan) and stand (modern stand). Compare German Zustand.

tsaar.e: Borrowed from Russian царь (carʹ), from Old East Slavic цьсарь (cĭsarĭ), from Proto-Slavic *cěsařь, from a Germanic language, from Proto-Germanic *kaisaraz, from Latin Caesar.

titaan.e: Borrowed from German Titan, named after Titania, a moon of Uranus.

telluur.e: From Latin tellurium, from the Roman tellurian deity Tellus.

examples: De toonhoogte van de fluit is hoger dan die van de tuba.
Zangers moeten hun toonhoogte controleren om zuiver te zingen.

s.def: A fast-growing or prosperous, usually Asian economy; usually located in East Asia or Southeast Asia but formerly also used for Ireland.
A fanatic, enthusiastic or otherwise highly motivated person.

tijger.e: From Middle Dutch tiger, from Old French tigre, from Latin tigris.

examples: Er ontbreekt iets in de cake. Hij is niet zoet genoeg. Heb je wel suiker toegevoegd?

f: te veel hooi op de vork nemen (alternative)

s.def: alternative form of te veel hooi op zijn vork nemen
a village in Oss, North Brabant, Netherlands

teeffelen.e: First attested as tefle in 1302. Potentially related to tafel (“table”) or Latin tabula (“table”); both terms have interpretations ranging from "table for exchanging money" and "market stall" to "moveable altar" or "square plot of land".

s.def: a hamlet in Land van Cuijk, North Brabant, Netherlands

toven.e: First attested as toeven in 1427. Etymology unknown.

s.def: a hamlet in Gemert-Bakel, North Brabant, Netherlands

tereyken.e: First attested as ter eyken in 1421. Derived from ter (“at”) and eik (“oak, Quercus sp.”).

s.def: trawler (fishing boat using a trawl net or dragnet)
a hamlet in Waalre, North Brabant, Netherlands

timmereind.e: First attested as Timmereind in 1748. Compound of dialectal timmer (“shack, house”) and eind (“edge, end”).

s.def: a neighbourhood of Valkenswaard, North Brabant, Netherlands
a hamlet in Veldhoven, North Brabant, Netherlands

toterfout.e: First attested as totevatsen akker in the 17th century. Etymology unclear. Perhaps derived from French tout fait (“all done”), referring to a piece of farmland that requires little work.

s.def: a hamlet in Bladel, North Brabant, Netherlands

ten vorsel.e: First attested as uersele around 1173. Derived from a compound of Middle Dutch varre (“young bull”) and Old Dutch sele (“hall home”). The element te (“at, near”) was added later. Compare Peursum, Poederoijen, Poeijers, Sterksel, Vaarle, Vaarsel, Varsen, Varssel, Varsselder, Varsseveld and Bolsele.

s.def: a hamlet in Hilvarenbeek, North Brabant, Netherlands
a hamlet in Oirschot, North Brabant, Netherlands

tregelaar.e: First attested as tregelaer in 1311. Compound of an unclear first element and laar (“intensively cultivated forest”). The first element has been variously interpreted as Medieval Latin tregula (“fencing”), Middle Dutch trege (“slow, languid”), Middle Dutch tregen (“to sadden”), Middle Dutch regel...

s.def: a neighbourhood and former municipality of Eindhoven, North Brabant, Netherlands

tongelre.e: First attested as tongerle in 1241. Potentially derived from a compound of Middle Dutch *tonger (“type of plant with a bitter taste”) and lo (“light forest”). Formerly an independent village.

s.def: a hamlet in Geldrop-Mierlo, North Brabant, Netherlands
a hamlet in Sint-Michielsgestel, North Brabant, Netherlands

tielse hoeve.e: First attested as de Tielenshoeve in 1653. Compound of Tielens (a surname) and hoeve (“farmstead”). The first element was gradually reinterpreted as Tiels (“pertaining to or from the city of Tiel”).

s.def: a neighbourhood of Sint-Michielsgestel, North Brabant, Netherlands

theereheide.e: First attested as Tederheijden in 1571. Compound of the toponym Thede (a lost settlement) and heide (“heath, heathland”).

s.def: a village and former municipality of Drimmelen, North Brabant, Netherlands

terheijden.e: First attested as van der heyden in 1332. Compound of an inflected form of te (“at”) and heide (“heath, heathland”).

s.def: a hamlet in Oosterhout, North Brabant, Netherlands

ter aalst.e: First attested as aelst in 1311. Derived from an inflected form of te (“at”) and a toponymic element of unknown etymology. The formerly widespread hypothesis of a derivation from els (“alder, Alnus”) with collectivising suffix -t is no longer considered plausible. Potentially derived from Proto-G...

s.def: a hamlet in Baarle-Nassau, North Brabant, Netherlands

tommel.e: First attested as tommelen in 1430. Derived either directly or indirectly from Latin tumba (“tomb, burial mound”). Compare Toom.

s.def: a hamlet in Alphen-Chaam, North Brabant, Netherlands

terover.e: First attested as van der over in 1348. Likely derived from a compound of an inflected form of te (“at”) and Middle Dutch over (“bank, shore”).

s.def: a village and former municipality of Breda, North Brabant, Netherlands

teteringen.e: First attested as apud tateringen in 1303. Likely derived from Middle Dutch tater (“mud, clot”). Compare Kloetinge.

s.def: a hamlet in Breda, North Brabant, Netherlands

tervoort.e: First attested as de voort in 1299. Univerbation of ter (“at the”) + voord(e) (“ford, crossing”).

s.def: a hamlet in Zundert, North Brabant, Netherlands

tiggelt.e: First attested as tichhelt around 1266-1268. Derived from Middle Dutch tiegele (“tile, paving stone”) suffixed with the collectivising suffix -t. The name was potentially given because of the presence of Roman building materials, which were repurposed by the locals.

s.def: a hamlet in Zundert, North Brabant, Netherlands

tiggeltscheberg.e: First attested as De Tichgelse Berge in 1534. Compound of the toponym Tiggelt and berg (“hill, elevated place”).

s.def: a neighbourhood of Roosendaal, North Brabant, Netherlands

tolberg.e: First attested as opden Tolberch in 1565. Compound of tol (“toll, tollpost”) and berg (“hill, elevated place”).

s.def: a hamlet in Moerdijk, North Brabant, Netherlands

tonnekreek.e: First attested as De ton creke in 1565. Derived from a hydronym, which is derived in turn from ton (“barrel”) and kreek (“creek, gully”). The river was named for the presence of a set of barrels used to mark it.

s.def: a hamlet in Goes, Zeeland, Netherlands

tervaten.e: Attested as VATE (TER) in 1848. Derived from a compound of an inflected form of te (“at”) and Zealandic vate (“water bowl, watering place for cattle”).

s.def: a village in Hulst, Zeeland, Netherlands

terhole.e: Attested as Ten Hole in 1542. Compound of an inflected form of te (“at”) and Middle Dutch hool (“floodgate with a stone bridge”).

s.def: a hamlet in Hulst, Zeeland, Netherlands

tasdijk.e: Attested as Tasdijk in 1899. Potentially a compound of the verb tassen (“stack, pile up”) and dijk (“levee”). Named after a nearby levee.

s.def: a city and municipality of Zeeland, Netherlands

terneuzen.e:
First attested as nose in 1340. Compound of an inflected form of te (“at”) and Middle Dutch nose (“headland, spit”).
See also Zealandic Neuzen.

s.def: a neighbourhood of Terneuzen, Zeeland, Netherlands

triniteit.e: Attested as van de poort van Triniteit around 1600. Derived from triniteit (“Holy Trinity”). Named after a church dedicated to the Holy Trinity.

s.def: a hamlet in Sluis, Zeeland, Netherlands

terhofstede.e:
First attested as polre qui dicitur hofstede around 1227. Compound of an inflected form of te (“at”) and hofstede (“piece of land (capable of) supporting a farmstead”). Originally the name of a polder.
See also Zealandic De Rostie.

s.def: a hamlet in Sluis, Zeeland, Netherlands

tragel.e: Attested as Tragel in 1740. Derived from Middle Dutch tragel (“quay, summer dike”).

s.def: a village in Noordoostpolder, Flevoland, Netherlands

tollebeek.e: First attested as Tollebeek in 1950. Named after a former village (first attested in 1364), the name of which derives from a compound of either Old Dutch Tolo (a personal name) or Middle Dutch tollen (“spin”) and beek (“brook, stream”).

s.def: a hamlet in Steenwijkerland, Overijssel, Netherlands

thij.e: First attested as tije in 1456. Derived from Middle Dutch tië (“meeting place in a village, place where law is spoken”).

s.def: a hamlet in Zwolle, Overijssel, Netherlands

tolhuislanden.e: First attested as Tolhuislanden in 1830-1855. Compound of tolhuis (“tollhouse”) and the plural form of land (“land”). Named after a former tollhouse located on the road to Nieuwleusen.

twenterand.e: Compound of the toponym Twente and rand (“edge”).

s.def: a neighbourhood of Raalte, Overijssel, Netherlands

tijenraan.e: First attested as tye en roiden around 1310. Compound of Middle Dutch tië (“meeting place in a village, place where law is spoken”) and a contraction of the dative plural form of Middle Dutch rode (“land cleared of trees”). Formerly an independent hamlet.

s.def: a hamlet in Deventer, Overijssel, Netherlands

tjoene.e: First attested as tgone in 1309. Etymology unknown. The toponym bears a passing resemblance to Old Saxon tiono (“shameful act”) and tuin (“garden”).

s.def: a neighbourhood of Borne, Overijssel, Netherlands
a hamlet in Almelo, Overijssel, Netherlands

tusveld.e: Potentially attested as thusvelde in 1262, attested with certainty as Het Tusveld in 1773-1794. Potentially a compound of dialectal tus (“thick layer of grass and weeds, peat”) or Old Dutch Tuet (a personal name) and veld (“field”).

s.def: a village and municipality of Overijssel, Netherlands

tubbergen.e: First attested as tubberg in 1312. Compound of te (“at”), Middle Dutch up (“on”) and the plural form of berg (“hill, elevated place”). Compare Taarlo, Tinallinge and Tolbert.

s.def: a village in Dinkelland, Overijssel, Netherlands

tilligte.e: First attested as tilgede in 1295. Derived from Middle Dutch telch (“young branch, young oak”) suffixed with the collectivising suffix -ede-. Compare Telgt.

s.def: a hamlet in Enschede, Overijssel, Netherlands

twekkelo.e: First attested as in tuegloe in the late 10th century. Potentially a compound of Old Dutch twek (“twig, osier”) and lo (“light forest”).

s.def: a neighbourhood of Enschede, Overijssel, Netherlands

twekkelerveld.e: First attested as Twekkeler Veld in 1867. Compound of the toponym Twekkelo and veld (“field”).

s.def: a hamlet in Leeuwarden, Friesland, Netherlands
a neighbourhood of Hengelo, Overijssel, Netherlands

tichelwerk.e:
* (Friesland) First attested as Wijnser Tichel Werk in 1718. Calque of West Frisian Tichelwurk, derived from tichelwurk (“brickworks”).
* (Overijssel) Derived from the archaic term tichelwerk (“brickworks”). The area which the current neighbourhood stands on was formerly occupied by brick factories.

s.def: a neighbourhood of Hengelo, Overijssel, Netherlands

tijertshoek.e: First attested as dat tije in 1493. Compound of a toponym composed of Middle Dutch tië (“public meeting place in a village, place where law is spoken”) and either Ard (a personal name) or the personifying suffix -aard and hoek (“isolated place”). Named after a former farm.

s.def: a neighbourhood of Steenwijkerland, Overijssel, Netherlands

torenlanden.e: Compound of toren (“tower”) and the plural form of land (“land”).

s.def: a neighbourhood of Moerdijk, North Brabant, Netherlands

torenveld.e: Compound of the toponym Toren (here referring to an area of reclaimed land) and veld (“field”). The first element may be a reference to the polder's location behind the one of the defensive towers of Zevenbergen, its tapering shape, or it may be a variant of doorn (“thorn bush”).

s.def: a neighbourhood of Breda, North Brabant, Netherlands
a neighbourhood of Horst aan de Maas, Limburg, Netherlands
a neighbourhood of Arnhem, Gelderland, Netherlands
a neighbourhood of Amsterdam, North Holland, Netherlands
a neighbourhood of Leiden, South Holland, Netherlands

transvaalbuurt.e: All compounds of the toponym Transvaal and buurt (“neighbourhood, quarter”). The neighbourhoods' streets are named after various South African figures.

s.def: a neighbourhood of Zutphen, Gelderland, Netherlands
a neighbourhood of Culemborg, Gelderland, Netherlands

terweijde.e: Compound of an inflected form of te (“at”) and weide (“pasture”).

s.def: a hamlet in De Wolden, Drenthe, Netherlands

ten arlo.e: First attested as in den narle in 1403. Compound of Middle Dutch *are (“maple, Acer sp.”) and lo (“light forest”). Compare Aarle, Aarle-Rixtel, Taarlo and Tynaarlo.

s.def: a village in Hoogeveen, Drenthe, Netherlands

tiendeveen.e:
First attested as het Tiendeveen in 1840. Compound of tiend (“type of tax”) and veen (“peat bog, marsh”).
See also Dutch Low Saxon Tiendeveine.

s.def: a neighbourhood of Hoogeveen, Drenthe, Netherlands
a neighbourhood of Emmen, Drenthe, Netherlands

tramwijk.e: Compound of tram (“tramway”) and wijk (“secondary canal in a turf extraction area”). Named after a nearby canal.

s.def: a village in Borger-Odoorn, Drenthe, Netherlands

tweede exloërmond.e:
First attested as Zuider Hoofddiep of Exlooder Mond in 1851-1855. Derived from tweede (“second”) and a compound of the toponym Exloo and mond (“small canal that links up with a primary canal”). Named after a nearby canal.
See also Dutch Low Saxon Twiede Ekselermond.

s.def: a hamlet in Borger-Odoorn, Drenthe, Netherlands

tweede valthermond.e:
First attested as Valthermond (Noord en Zuid) in 1899. Derived from tweede (“second”) and a compound of the toponym Valthe and mond (“small canal that links up with a primary canal”). Named after a nearby canal.
See also Dutch Low Saxon Twiede Valtermond.

s.def: a hamlet in Aa en Hunze, Drenthe, Netherlands

tweede dwarsdiep.e:
First attested as Dwarsdiep (Eerste en Tweede) in 1899. Derived from tweede (“second”) and a compound of dwars (“crossways”) and diep (“canal”). Named after a nearby canal.
See also Dutch Low Saxon Tweeide Dwarsdiep.

s.def: a hamlet in Aa en Hunze, Drenthe, Netherlands

torenveen.e: First attested as Torenveen in 1851-1855. Compound of toren (“tower”) and veen (“peat bog, marsh”). The bog near the settlement had a pointed shape, and was thus likened to a church tower.

s.def: a village in Tynaarlo, Drenthe, Netherlands

taarlo.e:
First attested as arlo in 820. Compound of Old Dutch *are (“maple”) and lo (“light forest”). The initial T- derives from a contraction of the preposition te (“at”) into the word. Compare Aarle, Aarle-Rixtel, Ten Arlo and Tynaarlo.
See also Dutch Low Saxon Toarl.

s.def: a hamlet in Noordenveld, Drenthe, Netherlands

terheijl.e: First attested as de helle in 1325. Derived from Middle Dutch hel (“low-lying land, pool, slope”). The toponym was gradually reinterpreted to hel (“hell”), and later to heil (“salvation”) in order to give it a more positive bent.

s.def: a hamlet in Assen, Drenthe, Netherlands

ter aard.e: First attested as in arth in 1335. Derived from an inflected form of te (“at”) and Middle Dutch aard (“tilled land, farmland”). Compare Aerdt, Eerde and Pannerden.

s.def: a village and former municipality of Groningen, Groningen, Netherlands

ten boer.e: First attested as bure in 1301. Derived from an inflected form of te (“at”) and Old Frisian buer (“house, cottage”).

s.def: a village in Groningen, Groningen, Netherlands

ten post.e: First attested as tenpost in 1380. Derived from an inflected form of te (“at”) and Middle Dutch post (“simple bridge”).

s.def: a village in Groningen, Groningen, Netherlands

thesinge.e: Potentially attested as thelingi in 786-787, attested with certainty as thiasingaclaster in the 13th century. Derived from Old Frisian *Thiasa (a personal name) suffixed with the collectivising suffix -ingi-.

s.def: a village in Midden-Groningen, Groningen, Netherlands

tjuchem.e: Potentially attested as thyuchen in 1319, attested with certainty as tijuchum in the 16th century. Derived from either West Frisian tjuch (“section of communal lands constantly redistributed under the farmers”) or Saterland Frisian tjüche (“place where cattle is kept, stock farm”).

s.def: a hamlet in Midden-Groningen, Groningen, Netherlands

tusschenloegen.e: First attested as TUSSCHENLOEGEN in 1848. Compound of tussen (“between”) and Dutch Low Saxon loug (“village”). The name of the settlement refers to its position between Muntendam and Zuidbroek.

s.def: a hamlet in Midden-Groningen, Groningen, Netherlands

tussenklappen.e: First attested as Tusschenklappen in 1867. Compound of tussen (“between”) and the plural form of klap (“drawbridge”). Compare Tusschen de Bruggen.

s.def: a village in Veendam, Groningen, Netherlands

tripscompagnie.e: First attested as Tripscompagnie in 1781. Compound of the surname Trip and compagnie (“company”). The settlement was founded by the company of businessman Adriaen Trip (1620-1684).

s.def: a hamlet in Stadskanaal, Groningen, Netherlands

ter wupping.e: First attested as Wippinge in 1545. Likely derived from an inflected form of te (“at”) and Old Frisian Wippe (a personal name) suffixed with the collectivising suffix -ingi-.

s.def: a hamlet in Stadskanaal, Groningen, Netherlands

ter maarsch.e: First attested as Ter marsch in 1634. Derived from an inflected form of te (“at”) and a variant of meers (“land next to water, swamp, pasture”).

s.def: a village in Westerwolde, Groningen, Netherlands

ter apelkanaal.e: First attested as Stads ter Apel Kanaal in 1867. Compound of the toponym Ter Apel and kanaal (“canal”).

s.def: a hamlet in Westerwolde, Groningen, Netherlands

ter haar.e: First attested as hare in 1474. Derived from an inflected form of te (“at”) and haar (“sandy ridge”).

s.def: a hamlet in Westerwolde, Groningen, Netherlands

ter wisch.e: First attested as de wisck in 1474. Derived from Middle Dutch wisch (“wet pasture, pasture which floods in winter”).

s.def: a hamlet in Westerwolde, Groningen, Netherlands

ter borg.e: First attested as der borch in 1474. Derived from an inflected form of te (“at”) and a variant of burg (“fortified place, castle”).

s.def: a hamlet in Westerwolde, Groningen, Netherlands

ter walslage.e: First attested as ter walslaghe in 1446. Derived from an inflected form of te (“at”) and a compound of an unclear first element and Dutch Low Saxon lage (“low-lying area”). The first element in Walslage may be a personal name. It is unlikely to be wal (“levee, wall”) or the hapax legomenon Middle...

s.def: a hamlet in Westerwolde, Groningen, Netherlands

tjabbesoord.e: First attested as Tjabbesoord in 1899. Compound of Tjabbes (a surname) and oord (“place”). Originally the name of a farmhouse, named in turn after its owner.

s.def: a village and former municipality of Eemsdelta, Groningen, Netherlands

termunten.e: First attested as in menterne in the 13th century. Derived from an inflected form of te (“at”) and the hydronym Munte. See also Muntendam.

s.def: a village in Eemsdelta, Groningen, Netherlands

termunterzijl.e: First attested as Zijl Ter Munte around 1660. Compound of the toponym Termunten and zijl (“discharge sluice”).

s.def: a village in Eemsdelta, Groningen, Netherlands

tjamsweer.e: First attested as in thiamerswerve in the 13th century. Compound of Old Frisian Thiadmâr (a personal name) and werve (“artificial mound for habitation”).

s.def: a village in Eemsdelta, Groningen, Netherlands

toornwerd.e: First attested as thornuurð in the 10th or 11th century. Compound of Old Frisian thorn (“thorn, thorn bush”) and uurð (“terp, artificial mound used for habitation”).

s.def: a hamlet in Eemsdelta, Groningen, Netherlands

tweehuizen.e: First attested as Tweehuizen in 1851-1855. Compound of twee (“two”) and the plural form of huis (“house”). The toponym contrasts with Vierhuizen.

s.def: a neighbourhood of Eemsdelta, Groningen, Netherlands

tuikwerd.e: First attested as tukawerth in the 13th century. Compound of Old Frisian *Tuka (a personal name) and werth (“terp, artificial mound used for habitation”). Formerly an independent village.

s.def: a village in Het Hogeland, Groningen, Netherlands

tinallinge.e: First attested as ingaddingenheim around the first half of the 10th century. Compound of Old Frisian Ingald (a personal name) and heem (“home, settlement”). The initial T- is the result of the preposition te (“at”) being contracted into the toponym. Compare Taarlo, Tolbert and Tubbergen.

s.def: a hamlet in Het Hogeland, Groningen, Netherlands
a hamlet in Het Hogeland, Groningen, Netherlands

ter laan.e: First attested as Terlaan in 1522. Derived from an inflected form of te (“at”) and Dutch Low Saxon laan (“lane, path used by a farmer to reach a plot of farmland while riding a horse-drawn carriage”).

s.def: a hamlet in Het Hogeland, Groningen, Netherlands

tijum.e: First attested as tihem in the 10th or 11th century. Compound of either Old Frisian *ti (“place where law is spoken”) or Tia (a personal name) and heem (“home, settlement”).

s.def: a hamlet in Westerkwartier, Groningen, Netherlands
a neighbourhood of Pijnacker-Nootdorp, South Holland, Netherlands

tolhek.e:
* (Groningen): Derived from tolhek (“toll-gate”).
* (Zuid-Holland): Derived from tolhek (“toll-gate”).

s.def: a hamlet in Westerkwartier, Groningen, Netherlands

topweer.e: First attested as Topweer in 1781. Compound of an unclear first element and Old Frisian were (“parcel, landed property”). The first element may be a familiar form of Old Frisian Theudobald (a personal name) or derive from Dutch Low Saxon top (“first cut of mown grass”).

s.def: a hamlet in Westerkwartier, Groningen, Netherlands

trimunt.e: First attested as trimont in 1473. Derived from a compound of Latin tres (“three”) and mons (“mountain, hill”). Named after a former Benedictine nunnery.

s.def: a former municipality of Friesland, Netherlands

tjum.e: Borrowed from an archaic variant of the toponym West Frisian Tsjom.

s.def: a village in Achtkarspelen, Friesland, Netherlands

twijzel.e: First attested as twislum around 1240. Borrowed from West Frisian Twizel, derived in turn from an Old Frisian term meaning "crossroads" (compare Old English *twisel (“split”) and Old High German zwisila (“forked branch”)).

s.def: a village in Achtkarspelen, Friesland, Netherlands

twijzelerheide.e: First attested as heyde in 1718. Calque of West Frisian Twizelerheide, a compound of the toponym Twizel and heide (“heath, heathland”).

s.def: a village and former municipality of Noardeast-Fryslân, Friesland, Netherlands

ternaard.e: First attested as tunuwerde around 819-825. Borrowed from West Frisian Ternaard. Originally a compound of an unclear first element and Old Frisian werd (“terp, artificial mound used for habitation”). The toponym was gradually reinterpreted to a compound of an inflected form of te (“at”) and aard ...

s.def: a village in Noardeast-Fryslân, Friesland, Netherlands

triemen.e: First attested as trema in 1467. Borrowed from West Frisian De Trieme, derived in turn from Old Frisian *trame (“beam, gangboard across a body of water”).

s.def: a hamlet in Noardeast-Fryslân, Friesland, Netherlands

teerd.e: First attested as Taert in 1511. Borrowed from West Frisian Teard, potentially derived in turn from a personal name and Old Frisian werd (“terp, artificial mound used for habitation”). Compare Kaard and Tjaard.

s.def: a hamlet in Noardeast-Fryslân, Friesland, Netherlands

teijeburen.e: First attested as Teijebuurt in 1899. Calque of West Frisian Teiebuorren, derived from a compound of Teije (a personal name) and the plural form of buer (“house, cottage”).

s.def: a hamlet in Noardeast-Fryslân, Friesland, Netherlands

tergracht.e: First attested as Greft in 1508. Calque of West Frisian Tergrêft, derived from a compound of an inflected form of te (“at”) and Old Frisian greft (“excavated canal”).

s.def: a hamlet in Noardeast-Fryslân, Friesland, Netherlands

ter lune.e: First attested as Ter Luyne in 1664. Borrowed from West Frisian Ter Lune.

s.def: a hamlet in Noardeast-Fryslân, Friesland, Netherlands

tibma.e: First attested as tippencheim around 822-856. Derived from West Frisian Tibbema (a surname). The current Frisian form of the toponym is derived from a significantly older variant.

s.def: a hamlet in Noardeast-Fryslân, Friesland, Netherlands

tilburen.e: First attested as Tilbuyren in 1664. Calque of West Frisian Tilbuorren, a compound of tille (“fixed bridge”) and the plural form of buer (“house, cottage”).

s.def: a hamlet in Noardeast-Fryslân, Friesland, Netherlands

tiltjeburen.e: Calque of West Frisian Tiltsjebuorren, a compound of a diminutive form of tille (“fixed bridge”) and the plural form of buer (“home, cottage”).

s.def: a hamlet in Leeuwarden, Friesland, Netherlands

tjeintgum.e: First attested as szyntinge in 1329. Borrowed from West Frisian Tsjeintgum, derived in turn from Old Frisian *Szynta (a personal name) suffixed with the collectivising suffix -ingi- and hēm (“home, settlement”). Compare Brantgum and Mantgum.

s.def: a hamlet in Leeuwarden, Friesland, Netherlands
a hamlet in Leeuwarden, Friesland, Netherlands

truurd.e: First attested as Truyrd in 1540. Borrowed from West Frisian Truerd, derived in turn from a compound of Old Frisian triu (“tree”) and werth (“artificial mound used for habitation”).

s.def: a village in Leeuwarden, Friesland, Netherlands

teerns.e: Potentially attested as terynse in 1397, attested with certainty as thernze in 1427. Borrowed from West Frisian Tearns, presumably derived from Old Frisian There (a personal name) suffixed with the collectivising suffix -ingi-. Compare Tirns.

s.def: a hamlet in Leeuwarden, Friesland, Netherlands

tjaard.e: First attested as tytzaerd in 1490. Borrowed from West Frisian Tsjaard, derived in turn from a compound of Old Frisian Tyts (a personal name) and werd (“artificial mound used for habitation”). Compare Kaard and Teerd.

s.def: a village in Opsterland, Friesland, Netherlands

terwispel.e: First attested as wispolia in 1315. Borrowed from West Frisian Terwispel, derived in turn from an inflected form of te (“at”) and the hydronym Wispel, potentially derived from a compound of Old Frisian wisch (“wet pasture”) and pôl (“pool”) or from a verb cognate to Dutch kwispelen (“wave, meande...

s.def: a village in Opsterland, Friesland, Netherlands

tijnje.e: First attested as Tinie in 1718. Borrowed from West Frisian De Tynje, derived in turn from tynje (“fence, fish weir made of withes”).

s.def: a village in Heerenveen, Friesland, Netherlands

terband.e: First attested as bant in 1315. Calque of West Frisian Terbant, derived in turn from a compound of an inflected form of te (“at”) and Old Saxon bant (“area, inhabited area”).

s.def: a village and former municipality of Heerenveen, Friesland, Netherlands

tjalleberd.e: First attested as tyanlaberde in 1315. Derived from a compound of Old Frisian Tjalle (a personal name) and the dative singular form of bert (“place with houses”). The Dutch version of the toponym was borrowed from an earlier form of the Frisian name.

terwisscha.e: First attested as Terwisch and Ter wijsga in 1565. Compound of an inflected form of te (“at”) and wisch (“wet pasture”).

And although it is now a neuter noun, at least as recently as the fifteenth century Middle Dutch ore could still have a feminine grammatical gender, giving some indication as to...

De Indiaan^([sic]) echter zegt u, dat die klei afkomstig van de groote bakkerij, welke Tamusi er eenmaal op nahield, toen hij uit alino of alina, klei of leem, bezig was kalina, menschen te maken.
Het eerste uit de grondbegrippen door Tamoesji opgedacht wezen (schepsel) is „wo“ of het eerst^([sic – meaning eerste]) woord = „wo“, dit beteekent mensch (wo-kili = man; wo-li = vrouw).

trekken aan een dood paard.e: Literally pulling on a dead horse. While pulling on a live horse may result in the horse starting to walk in the direction it's being pulled in, a dead horse will never contribute to the effort to move in a particular direction.

telkenmale.e: Univerbation of te (“at”) + elken (“each”) + male (“occasion”), where te governs the dative. The dative male is reduced to maal in the alternative form telkenmaal, and in telkenmaals an adverbial -s is added.

tjeerd.e: Borrowed from West Frisian Tjeerd, Tjaard, from Old Frisian Thiadward and/or Thiadhard, containing the Germanic elements *þeudaz (“people”) and either *wardaz (“ward”) or *harduz (“hard, brave”). Compare German Dietward, Diethard.

touteren.e: First attested in the early sixteenth century and thus not attested in Middle Dutch and Old Dutch, but ultimately inherited from Proto-Germanic *taltrōną; compare Old English tealtrian. The shift from -alt- to -out- is the result of Middle Dutch L-vocalization.

Het onkruid komt tussen de tegels uit.
Hij ging bij zijn mentor te rade.

‘Wijsheid’ is de term die hij kleeft op de kennis die nodig is om existentiële vragen te beantwoorden: wie zijn wij? Waar komen wij vandaan? Waar gaan wij naartoe? We zijn vergeten, stelt Verhaeghe, dat ook die vragen het onderwerp zijn van wetenschappelijk onderzoek. Maar om ze te kunnen beantwoorden hebben we andere methodes nodig en gaan we beter te rade bij kunst, literatuur en religie. Bij de klassieken waren die vakgebieden nog grotendeels versmolten, en ook wetenschap maakte deel uit van die cluster.

te rade gaan.e: Contains a fossilized dative case form rade, as the dative was historically used with the preposition te (see usage notes there). The entire expression may be translated literally as "to go for counsel".
